title: Rainbow Disco Science with Uncle Grisco
summary: Disco, Disco, and Papa Mole embark on a trip to the home of Uncle Grisco, a dedicated science teacher. Amidst discussions of the magical Rainbow Disco Unicorns from their favorite Dingo-O songs, Uncle Grisco explains the true nature of rainbows. He teaches the visitors how white sunlight is split into a colorful spectrum by water droplets and brainstorms scientific ways to create rainbows using prisms, garden hoses, and creative artwork.

highlightQuote.text: Oh, I don't know, but it also has orange and then it has the jump.

transcript.text: Can you tell us a story about Uncle Grisco and Ding Dong?
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Who is it? say Disco, Disco.
Hi Papa Mole!
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: Hi Papa Mole! We're here to tell a story about Uncle Grisco.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Ding dong!
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Who is it? say the moles.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: No, no, no, not the moles, no Uncle Grisco!
It's Uncle Grisco who arrives to visit the moles.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, the mole having the trip to his house.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Oh, this time Disco and Disco and Papa Mole are visiting Uncle Grisco's house.
And the cool thing about Uncle Grisco's house, since he's a science teacher, is he has lots of cool science stuff.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Now, what is an interesting science experiment that Uncle Grisco could do with the moles today? Hmm.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: And the moles arrive at Uncle Grisco's house.
And they arrive and they open the door and Uncle Grisco says: "Welcome moles! I have a house full of science experiments. Are you curious about any science things that you want to learn about?"
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Huh, I'm curious about Rainbow Disco Unicorns.
Rainbow Disco Unicorns? What are those?
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Those are just something from Dingo-O.
Oh, like from a song, I see.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Yeah, and they're on my go-go.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: I see, that's very interesting, says Uncle Grisco. Now, Rainbow Disco Unicorns are pretend, but rainbows are real. Do you want to learn about rainbows?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Oh yeah, Rainbow Disco Unicorns. There's also disco in that word.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: There's also disco in that word. That's true. Disco is a kind of music that people dance to.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: And also there's unicorn.
Do you know what a unicorn is?
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Yeah.
What is it?
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Oh, I don't know, but it also has orange and then it has the jump.
It has the jump? Oh. Well, a unicorn is a magical kind of horse that's pretend, that has a horn on its head, one horn.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Yeah, and it also has the rainbows, has disco too. Can't forget those parts.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: I see. Well, in that song it combines all those ideas. But one of the ideas in how we do science is to take things apart to help us understand them.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: It might need a computer to help us.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: We might? What do you think we would do with the computer?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Uncle... so we also we might need to do some research about Dingo-O.
That's true. Yeah, about Dingo-O music that he does.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Do you have any questions about that that you want to do research about?

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Okay, actually I do have... so we just need to listen to it and find out.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Oh, listening is a part of science, listening to instructions.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: That's true, it is. So we can listen to it in the morning.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Good idea. But for now, we could think just about one of those three things. Do you want to think just about rainbows, just about disco, or just about unicorns?
Rainbows.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Rainbows. Now, can you describe a rainbow?
Yeah. Rainbow has red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: That's correct! And did you know that rainbows can appear in the sky?
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: After a rain.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: After a rain, that's right. Have you seen one in the sky after a rain?
No, there was never a rain or a rainbow after a rain at my house.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: I think we should keep our eyes open for rainbows after rain, says Uncle Grisco, 'cause I love seeing rainbows. But they don't last for very long, so you really gotta look out for them at just the right time. And do you know what makes a rainbow? asked Uncle Grisco.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: No.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: The rainbow is made by light getting split apart into the different colors. The light from the sun comes through the air and it's all bright white. And normally the sunlight just lights everything up with its white light. But a rainbow happens when there's little droplets of water in the air. And those droplets of water make the light split apart into its different colors. And the red goes one way, the orange goes a different way, the yellow and the green and the blue and the purple, they all come out in different directions, and so they split apart.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Also the pink, also the indigo, and also the violet.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: That's correct. The light gets split apart into its different colors.
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: Yeah, I love rainbows. Also black, also black and brown and white.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Yeah, those are colors too, but we don't see them in rainbows. Do you have any other questions about rainbows?

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Oh, in that song there is a little bit of singing and a little bit of humming and something else that we sing, more humming too.
Yeah, in Rainbow Disco Unicorn?
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: Yeah, it's the orange, it's the orange part.
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: How does it go?
Orange up up and down, now that's how it goes.
transcript.speaker: Izzy

transcript.text: Well, I can't wait to hear it tomorrow morning, says Uncle Grisco. And now, let's do one science experiment. I wonder if we could make a rainbow. Hmm. There are a couple ways we could make a rainbow. One way is to use a little object called a prism. That is a special clear piece of glass that has a shape that makes the light bend when it goes through it and it splits apart the light into different colors. And so you can make your own little rainbow by shining a light through it onto a table. That's one way to make a rainbow.
transcript.speaker: Dad

transcript.text: What's the other ways?
transcript.speaker: Izzy
Dad
transcript.text: Well, another way is, sometimes, you know, we see rainbows in the sky after a rain because of the droplets of water in the air. Sometimes if we just spray a hose just right, you can make lots of little droplets right in your own yard. And then the light from the sun makes a tiny rainbow right in your yard. That's another way to make a rainbow. Hmm, I wonder what some other ways are.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Drawing a picture of a rainbow.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: We can definitely make a rainbow by drawing a picture, that's true. That's a wonderful way to do it. Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: So we could paint, paint a rainbow.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Yeah, we could paint a rainbow or use markers.

transcript.speaker: Izzy
transcript.text: Or we could do a work of rainbows.
Oh, what would we do in the work of rainbows?
transcript.speaker: Dad
Izzy
transcript.text: So we can put little rainbows from one cup to another cup. That's going to be a work of rainbows.

transcript.speaker: Dad
transcript.text: Oh, pouring the rainbows from one cup to another cup? That sounds really pretty. Wow, I wonder how you'd pour a rainbow. Oh, that's our timer. So that's the end of Stars and Stories. Okay.

characters.name: Uncle Grisco
characters.description: A nomadic mole scientist and science teacher, this subterranean inventor resides in a home brimming with wondrous experiments, serving as a devoted confidant to the gopher folk. Within his laboratory of glowing elixirs and prismatic shadows, he fuses volcanic lore with the study of light to translate the planet's restless movements and the scarred history of cosmic craters. A wandering sage who maps both deep tectonic veins and celestial paths, he frequently emerges from his sprawling tunnels to share ancient botanical secrets and astronomical insights with his friend Seeker.

characters.name: Disco
characters.description: An energetic Mischief Mole and superhero-clad detective, Disco is one of two identical twins who embark on grand adventures through the deep. Alongside her sister Thisco, this architect of the subterranean maps hidden realms—from frozen frontiers to the dusty canyons beneath the furniture—by piloting her signature submarine or racing toy cars through the bedroom's wild heights. Guided by "Cornebyborn" dances and Papa Mole’s lore, she finds solace within her glowing basket house, a dream sanctuary and fortress of a hundred locks. Whether navigating grimy domestic landscapes or open waters, Disco balances mechanical ingenuity with a persistent, soft-hearted wonder for the night sky.

characters.name: Papa Mole
characters.description: The wise leader and industrious architect of the Mischief Moles, this polite elder visionary guides his kin from the attic’s shadowed heights toward the stars and across biting, crystalline ice. A master pathfinder who initiated their voyage upon unearthing an ancient tome, he balances the weight of celestial navigation with the tactile craft of carving a new tunnel home and the thoughtful devotion of planning whimsical days of activities for his children. Whether introducing surprise robots for play, anchoring meditations during a frozen trek, or sleep-sitting in his favorite chair, his tactical brilliance ensures their furthest discoveries remain rooted in the rhythmic, quiet peace of a sanctuary built by his own seasoned hands.

characters.name: Rainbow Disco Unicorns
characters.description: Pretend magical creatures featured in a song by Dingo-O.

places.name: Uncle Grisco's House
places.description: Uncle Grisco’s subterranean home is a sprawling burrow and sophisticated laboratory where local moles frequently congregate. Within these earthen walls, cozy domesticity meets the hum of progress, as tangled roots frame glowing vials and intricate machinery dedicated to clandestine experiments and scientific discovery.

places.name: Dingo-O
places.description: The place or artist that creates songs about Rainbow Disco Unicorns.
